narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation, but we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice for our law program. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on your first attempt, and unfortunately, it estimated an 82% chance of failure for you, which is the threshold that led to denying your application to protect both you and our program's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by two key factors from your profile: your LSAT score and your Year 3 school decile ranking based on grades. Your LSAT score of 24 stood out as the strongest influence pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, as it falls well below the typical applicant's score of around 37, signaling potential challenges in the rigorous reasoning demands of law school and the bar. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 2—much lower than the average of about 5.5—reinforced this concern, suggesting inconsistent academic performance later in your schooling compared to most peers who rank higher. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, while below the average of 3.21, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, showing some resilience in your college work. Your middle-income background and full-time enrollment plans also offered small boosts toward a passing prediction, aligning with many successful applicants. Overall, compared to typical students in our dataset—who boast stronger LSATs and higher decile rankings—your profile appeared riskier for first-time bar passage, tipping the scales against admission. We truly empathize with this outcome, especially knowing the dedication it takes as a white male from a middle-class family to pursue law. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 37 or higher, bolstering your later-year grades through additional coursework, and perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or bar prep resources tailored to your strengths. Reapplying after strengthening these areas could significantly improve your odds, and many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing here. We're rooting for your success in law—keep pushing forward.
